FM-GPT Tutorial

Data Generation

We will first generate a synthetic dataset using the included functions. For this example, we will consider a set of 10 continuous outcomes which can be represented by 3 factors. We will generate \(n_1=100\) samples in the eQTL reference dataset and \(n_2=1000\) samples in the GWAS dataset. We will simulate a singular region of 10 genes, each gene containing 10 cis-SNPs. The LD matrix for the SNPs will be \(\rho_{\text{within}}=0.66\) and \(\rho_{\text{between}}=0.33\). We will assume approximately 1 gene is causal of the 10 in this region, with a gene expression heritability of 10%. We will also specify the phenotype heritability of approximately 5%.

set.seed(1423)
dat <- gen_twas_sets(n_qtl = 100, n_gwas = 5000, n_genes = 10, snps_per_gene = 10, block_sizes = rep(10, 10), rho_between = 0.33, rho_within = 0.66, response_types = c(10, 0, 0), k = 3, maf = 0.24, choice = "genes", sig_rho = 0.66, pi0 = 1, pi1 = 0.1, pi2 = 1, expression_heritability = 0.1,sigma2 = 1, binflate = 0.05)

The dat object contains the following elements:

names(dat)
##  [1] "G_qtl"     "G_gwas"    "E_qtl"     "SNP_coef"  "Yl"        "Yo"       
##  [7] "Lambda"    "Sigma"     "group"     "A"         "beta"      "r"        
## [13] "responses" "U"         "Ucov"      "LD"

The most important elements are: G_qtl and G_gwas, which contain the genotype data for the eQTL and GWAS studies, E_qtl, which contains the gene expression data for the eQTL study, Yl and Yo which contain the latent factors and observed outcome data, Lambda, which contains the loading matrix, beta which contains the gene causality pattern for the latent factors, and finally responses, which categorizes the response type of the outcome data (e.g. continuous, binary, count).

Now let’s view the gene causality pattern.

dat$beta
##            [,1]      [,2]      [,3]
##  [1,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [2,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [3,] 0.1715735 0.3307978 0.1299771
##  [4,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [5,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [6,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [7,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [8,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
##  [9,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000
## [10,] 0.0000000 0.0000000 0.0000000

Computation

Now we shall estimate the predicted expression from the synthetic data. We will use a spike-and-slab regression implementation.

est_coef_mat <- matrix(nrow = nrow(dat$SNP_coef), ncol = ncol(dat$SNP_coef))

for(j in 1:ncol(dat$SNP_coef)) {
  tmp_out <- lm.spike(scale(dat$E_qtl[,j], scale = FALSE) ~ scale(dat$G_qtl, scale = FALSE)+0, niter = 1000)
  est_coef_mat[,j] <- apply(tmp_out$beta[751:1000,], 2, median)
}

For example, for the first gene, here is the weight vector for its 10 SNPs:

est_coef_mat[1:10,1]
##  [1]  0.000000  0.000000  0.000000  0.000000 -1.719266  0.000000  0.000000
##  [8]  0.000000  0.000000 -1.101098

Now, we will generate the predicted gene expressions and fit the model.

E_gwas <- dat$G_gwas %*% est_coef_mat
fit <- FMGPT(X = scale(E_gwas, scale = FALSE), Y = scale(dat$Yo, scale = FALSE), A = 0*dat$A, group = seq(1, 10), response_types = dat$responses, epsilon = 0.4, B = 3000, burnin = 2000, thin = 1, k_fix = NULL, scale.eta = FALSE)

We can first assess the total number of factors the model has suggested exist in the data:

ts.plot(fit$kstar, xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "Number of Factors")

Additionally, we can view the loading matrix pattern. Based on the trace plot of the number of factors, we will view the loadings for the first six factors:

infinitefactor::plotmat(t(apply(fit$lambda, c(2, 3),mean, na.rm = TRUE))[,1:6])

We can also do more classic methods of factor determination, such as the correlation plot and the scree plot:

corrplot::corrplot(cor(dat$Yo))

psych::scree(cor(dat$Yo), pc = FALSE)

Furthermore, we can try to suppress certain loadings to zero. Looking at the posterior mean of the loading matrix for the first three factors, we have:

t(apply(fit$lambda, c(2, 3),mean, na.rm = TRUE))[,1:3]
##               [,1]         [,2]         [,3]
##  [1,]  0.228136006 -0.021832330 -0.188681266
##  [2,]  1.678967752 -0.421212221 -0.026450774
##  [3,] -1.975485138 -0.720422383  1.050560210
##  [4,] -0.275308863  0.027476766  0.112662100
##  [5,]  0.208116483  0.127676866 -0.068475715
##  [6,]  0.312933988  0.765620239 -0.387355095
##  [7,] -0.003455489  0.009329259  0.002512951
##  [8,] -0.447556340  0.106884653  0.345457250
##  [9,]  0.719770599 -0.270749420  0.131224191
## [10,]  0.471726854 -0.027442417 -0.529810597

Now, we’ll apply the post-hoc unit scaling adjustment to determine which loadings to keep within each factor.

dim(fit$lambda)
## [1] 1000   10   10
lambda_draws <- fit$lambda[,1:3,]
for(b in 1:750) {
  for(k in 1:3) {
    lambda_draws[b,k,] <- lambda_draws[b,k,] / sqrt(sum(lambda_draws[b,k,]^2))
  }
}
t(apply(lambda_draws, c(2, 3),mean, na.rm = TRUE))[,1:3]
##               [,1]          [,2]         [,3]
##  [1,]  0.125128803 -0.0237267830 -0.108230296
##  [2,]  0.871217714 -0.3160903453 -0.037762536
##  [3,] -1.103193680 -0.5374466405  0.314718766
##  [4,] -0.142874434  0.0007746511  0.073747340
##  [5,]  0.106915864  0.1040648931  0.005468073
##  [6,]  0.193282812  0.5339733095  0.071825144
##  [7,] -0.004489443 -0.0034926657  0.018296691
##  [8,] -0.256985029  0.0552749793  0.273067655
##  [9,]  0.365444884 -0.2025981749  0.021096815
## [10,]  0.267596961 -0.0043584861 -0.364186470

This matrix gives the relative magnitude of each loading in each factor. We’ll keep only those loadings with an absolute magnitude > 0.10.

lambda_keep <- t(apply(lambda_draws, c(2, 3),mean, na.rm = TRUE))[,1:3]
lambda_keep[abs(lambda_keep) < 0.1] <- 0
t(apply(fit$lambda, c(2, 3),mean, na.rm = TRUE))[,1:3] * (abs(lambda_keep) > 0.10)
##             [,1]       [,2]       [,3]
##  [1,]  0.2281360  0.0000000 -0.1886813
##  [2,]  1.6789678 -0.4212122  0.0000000
##  [3,] -1.9754851 -0.7204224  1.0505602
##  [4,] -0.2753089  0.0000000  0.0000000
##  [5,]  0.2081165  0.1276769  0.0000000
##  [6,]  0.3129340  0.7656202  0.0000000
##  [7,]  0.0000000  0.0000000  0.0000000
##  [8,] -0.4475563  0.0000000  0.3454572
##  [9,]  0.7197706 -0.2707494  0.0000000
## [10,]  0.4717269  0.0000000 -0.5298106
infinitefactor::plotmat(t(apply(fit$lambda, c(2, 3),mean, na.rm = TRUE))[,1:3] * (abs(lambda_keep) > 0.10))

Finally, now that we’ve determined to keep 3 factors, we can look at the omnibus PIP of the genes for these three factors:

plot(twas_pip(fit, 3), xlab = "gene", ylab= "PIP", col = dat$A+1, pch = 19)

Clearly the gene with the highest PIP is the 3rd gene, which matches the gene causality pattern shown above.
